E.4 Disentangling the sources of aggregation error For the two subplots in Figures 4a and 4b, we use the depth-four binary conditioning tree described in Appendix E.3. For visual clarity, Figure 4a shows only the first three levels. Node-wise conditional alignment. For Figure 4a, we evaluate the quality of the LLM-estimated conditional income distributions at each node of the tree. The outcome is the four-bin income distribution used in Figure 2. For every node S, we compare the LLM-estimated conditional distribution ˆTh(S) to the corresponding survey-based conditional distribution Th(S) using the normalized Wasserstein distance, 1( ˆTh(S), Th(S)). The node color encodes this error. The resulting plot illustrates that conditioning on more specific subgroups improves conditional alignment relative to the root estimate, but that the improvement is heterogeneous across the tree. Some fine-grained subgroups with negligible prior mass remain poorly aligned. Prior alignment. For Figure 4b, we evaluate the quality of the subgroup priors. For each tree level ℓ, let γℓ denote the survey-based distribution over the partition Pℓ(X ), and let ˆγℓ denote the corresponding LLM-estimated prior distribution obtained with the elicitation procedure from Appendix E.2. We measure prior alignment using total variation distance, TV(ˆγℓ, γℓ). This yields one prior-alignment error per model and tree level. The results show that prior estimation becomes increasingly difficult as 36 the tree is refined. Coarse partitions are estimated comparatively accurately, whereas deeper levels require the model to distribute probability mass across larger number of more specific subpopulations. Thus, aggregation involves trade-off. More specific conditioning can improve the subgroup outcome estimates, but the corresponding population weights become harder to estimate accurately. E.5 Variance decomposition across tree levels The variance decomposition in Figure 4c is computed on the depth-four binary conditioning tree described in Appendix E.3. For each node, we compute the survey-weighted subgroup prior γS , the conditional mean µS E[Y S], and the conditional variance V(Y S) from the ACS data. We then decompose the aggregate income variance into within-subgroup and cross-subgroup component. The within-subgroup term is the prior-weighted average conditional variance, Vwithin(ℓ) (cid:88) γS V(Y S), SPℓ(X ) which measures the residual heterogeneity that remains after conditioning on the attributes defining the level-ℓ subgroups. The cross-subgroup term is the prior-weighted variance of the subgroup means, Vbetween(ℓ) (cid:88) γS (µS µ)2 with µ (cid:88) γS µS . SPℓ(X ) SPℓ(X ) By the law of total variance, these quantities satisfy V(Y ) = Vwithin(ℓ) + Vbetween(ℓ) for every level ℓ, up to finite-sample estimation error. Thus, the two terms quantify how refinement shifts variation from residual within-subgroup heterogeneity to explicit cross-subgroup heterogeneity. E.6 Self-consistency evaluation on the binned ACS income setting For the self-consistency analysis in Table 1a, we use the binned ACS income prediction task from the running example in Figure 2. The model is asked to estimate the income distribution over the following four bins: Bin 1: [11.5k, 1) USD Bin 2: [1, 25k) USD Bin 3: [25k, 60k) USD Bin 4: [60k, 1849k] USD These bins cover the full income range observed in the ACS sample: no individual in the ACS sample reports an income below 11.5k USD or above 1849k USD. The results in Table 1a report the two benchmark scores introduced in Section 5. The first is the split consistency score SCε(A) from Definition 3. For every admissible pair of conditioning event and next split attribute in CSC(A), we compare the models direct estimate at the conditioning event to the prior-weighted aggregate of its estimates at the two refinements induced by the split attribute. For the attribute set consisting of age and employment status, this yields six node-level checks. The second is the order consistency score OCε(A) from Definition 5. For each of the four two-attribute conditioning events in COC(A), we elicit direct estimates under both orderings of the attribute constraints and check whether the two predictions agree. Since the target is an ordered categorical distribution, discrepancies are measured using the normalized Wasserstein distance 1. Each score reports the fraction of checks whose discrepancy is less than the tolerance ε = 0.02. 37 E.7 Self-consistency evaluation on the thresholded ACS income setting For the model comparison in Figure 6, we use the thresholded ACS income prediction task with threshold τ = 40k USD. The setting mirrors the binned income experiment in Appendix E.6. We use the same conditioning tree over the attribute set consisting of age and employment status. The only change is the target variable, which is now the binary indicator of whether an individuals income exceeds τ . For each model, we compute the split consistency score SCε(A) and the order consistency score OCε(A) at tolerance ε = 0.02. Since the target is binary, the normalized Wasserstein distance 1 reduces to the absolute difference between the two estimated probabilities of exceeding the threshold. As proxy for general model capability, we report the Artificial Analysis Intelligence (AAI) Index [Artificial Analysis, 2026]. Models are grouped by family and ordered within each family by their AAI Index. E.8 Self-consistency evaluation on the ACS commute time setting For the self-consistency analysis in Table 1b, we use the binned ACS commute time prediction task. The setting mirrors the binned income experiment in Appendix E.6. We use the same conditioning tree over the attribute set consisting of age and employment status, and the same subgroup priors. The only change is the target variable, which is now the commute time in minutes. The model is asked to estimate the commute time distribution over the following four bins: Bin 1: [0, 1) minute Bin 2: [1, 15) minutes Bin 3: [15, 30) minutes Bin 4: [30, 195] minutes These bins cover the full range of commute times observed in the ACS sample. No individual reports commute time longer than 195 minutes. We compute the split consistency score SCε(A) and the order consistency score OCε(A) at tolerance ε = 0.02, exactly as in Appendix E.6. Since the target is an ordered categorical distribution, discrepancies are measured using the normalized Wasserstein distance 1. E.9 Self-consistency evaluation on WVS opinion distributions We evaluate self-consistency on opinion distributions derived from the World Values Survey (WVS). The experiment reported in Table 2 uses data from Canada and Indonesia. For both countries, the evaluation is based on the attribute set consisting of the following two splits: Level 1: Age is split into 16 44 years old and 45 103 years old. Level 2: Income is split into low and high. The interpretation of low and high in the income split is left to the model. This is sufficient for our self-consistency checks, provided that the two options are interpreted as complementary categories that form valid partition of the relevant population. For each country and each of the five selected WVS questions listed in Table 6, we elicit categorical response distributions at every conditioning event induced by A. The corresponding prompt template is given in Appendix G.5. Each question then defines distinct prediction problem, on which we compute the split consistency score SCε(A) and the order consistency score OCε(A) at tolerance ε = 0.02. For the split consistency score, we evaluate all admissible pairs of conditioning event and next split attribute in CSC(A), comparing the direct estimate at the conditioning event to the prior-weighted aggregate of its two refinements. For the order consistency score, we elicit direct estimates under both orderings of the attribute constraints for each of the four two-attribute conditioning events in COC(A) and check whether the two predictions agree. Discrepancies are measured using the normalized Wasserstein distance 1, and each score reports the fraction of checks whose discrepancy is less than ε = 0.02. E.10 Self-consistency evaluation of tennis forecasting example Consider forecasting task in which model is asked to estimate the probability that Roger Federer wins match against Rafael Nadal, conditional on contextual information available before or during the match. All estimates are elicited using the tennis forecasting prompt template in Appendix G.6. At the root node, the prompt provides only the matchup itself, corresponding to blind or unconditional forecast without additional information. We then gradually reveal pieces of contextual information that may become available before or during the match, such as the court surface. These conditions induce binary conditioning tree analogous to the BCTs used in the income prediction setting, but the conditioning variables now describe sporting event rather than demographic subpopulation. Concretely, the attribute set consists of two binary conditions: the court surface, distinguishing clay from non-clay courts, and the in-match event of whether Federer wins the first set. The former captures pre-match contextual factor that is highly relevant to the FedererNadal rivalry, while the latter represents partial information that becomes available during the match. The same self-consistency checks from Section 5 can then be applied, with the models estimated prior probabilities serving as aggregation weights. To generate the results in Table 3a, we compute the split consistency score SCε(A) and the order consistency score OCε(A) at tolerance ε = 0.02. Since the target outcome is binary, discrepancies are measured by the absolute difference between the corresponding win probabilities. Each score reports the fraction of checks whose discrepancy is less than ε = 0.02. E.11 Self-consistency evaluation of fantasy example As second synthetic example, we consider reasoning task in fictional fantasy role-playing game. The model is asked to estimate the probability that the main character wins an encounter against an opponent using the prompt template in Appendix G.7. Unlike the survey setting, and unlike the tennis example, this task need not correspond to any real-world data-generating process. The game world is imaginary, and there is no external ground-truth distribution against which the models estimates can be evaluated. This makes the example useful for highlighting key feature of our self-consistency checks. They only require the models own direct and conditional estimates, and therefore remain meaningful even when no reference distribution exists. At the root node, the prompt specifies only the basic combat situation. We then refine the scenario by revealing additional contextual information about the game world. The attribute set consists of two binary conditions: the time of day, distinguishing day and night, and the wind conditions, distinguishing calm from windy combat environments. To generate the results in Table 3b, we proceed exactly as in the tennis example (Appendix E.10). We compute the split consistency score SCε(A) over the admissible checks in CSC(A) and the order consistency score OCε(A) over the four two-attribute conditioning events in COC(A). Consistency requires the compared estimates to agree, even though the underlying event is fictional and no groundtruth win probability is available. Since the target outcome is binary, discrepancies are measured by the absolute difference between the corresponding win probabilities, and each score reports the fraction of checks whose discrepancy is less than ε = 0.02.

sections.title: G Prompt templates
sections.content: This appendix reports the exact prompt templates used to elicit probability estimates from language models. G.1 Prior elicitation

sections.title: Subgroup priors
sections.content: You are given mutually exclusive and exhaustive partition of the U.S. population into groups defined by sociodemographic attributes. For each group below, estimate the probability that randomly sampled person from the U.S. population belongs to that group. {groups} Important: - Treat the listed groups as the full population partition. - Assign probability mass across all groups. - The returned probabilities must sum to 1. - Use your best demographic knowledge of the U.S. population. Return only valid JSON object of the following form: { "Group 1": 0.35, "Group 2": 0. } Strict output rules: - Do not include any text outside the JSON object. - Do not include explanations or uncertainty intervals. - Each probability must be single number in [0, 1]. - The JSON object must contain exactly one entry per group. - Use the group names exactly as given: Group 1, Group 2. The groups placeholder is replaced by list of groups, each specified by structured list of sociodemographic attributes that define the corresponding subgroup in the partition. For example: Group 1 - sex: female Group 2 - sex: male G.2 Sociodemographic prompting Thresholded income task You are given brief sociodemographic profile of an individual. Based on the information provided and your general knowledge of income distributions in the United States, estimate the probability of the specified outcome. The data provided is sufficient to make an approximate probabilistic estimate. 43 Individual attributes: {attributes} Question: What is the estimated probability that this persons annual income exceeds {income_threshold} USD? Output requirements (strict): - Return single real number between 0 and 1 (inclusive). - Format the answer exactly as: [[p]] where is your probability estimate (for example: [[0.5]]). In the sociodemographic formulation, the attributes placeholder is replaced by structured list of demographic attributes describing the target individual. For example: Sex: Female Employment status: Civilian employed, at work This formulation describes the conditioning information in the third person and avoids asking the model to adopt role-playing perspective. G.3 Persona prompting Thresholded income task Please answer the following question from the perspective of the described person. Stay consistent with the profile, but do not mention that you are role-playing unless asked. Persona: {persona} Question: What is the estimated probability that this persons annual income exceeds {income_threshold} USD? Output requirements (strict): - Return single real number between 0 and 1 (inclusive). - Format the answer exactly as: [[p]] where is your probability estimate (for example: [[0.5]]). In the persona formulation, the persona placeholder is replaced by free-text description that conveys the same demographic information in first-person role-playing style. For example, the profile may read: You are person who lives in the United States. Your sex is Female. G.4 Micro-to-macro prompting Thresholded income task Based on your general knowledge of income distributions in the United States, estimate the probability of the specified outcome. Please make an approximate probabilistic estimate. Question: What is the estimated probability that randomly selected persons annual income exceeds {income_threshold} USD? 44 Before giving your final answer, reason from micro to macro: 1. Identify several relevant subpopulations within the U.S. population that may differ in income. 2. For each subpopulation, consider both its approximate prevalence in the U.S. population and its likely probability of exceeding the income threshold. 3. Combine these subgroup-level considerations into single aggregate estimate for the U.S. population. Output requirements (strict): - Return single real number between 0 and 1 (inclusive). - Format the answer exactly as: [[p]] where is your probability estimate (for example: [[0.5]]). This prompt asks the model to estimate population-level income probability by explicitly reasoning through relevant U.S. subpopulations, their prevalence, and their subgroup-specific probabilities. Unlike our tree-based aggregation prompts, the relevant subpopulations are not specified in advance but are instead chosen by the model. G.5 Opinion distribution elicitation Survey-response distribution prompt You are estimating the distribution of survey responses for demographic subpopulation. The subpopulation is characterized by the following attributes: {attribute_list} Survey question: {question} Answer options: {answer_option_list} Task: Estimate the probability distribution over the answer options for randomly selected individual from this subpopulation. Strict output rules: - Return only valid JSON. - Use exactly the following keys: {answer_option_keys}. - Each value must be single number in [0, 1]. - The values must sum to 1. - Use decimal probabilities, not percentages. - Do not include explanations, comments, uncertainty intervals, or any text outside the JSON object. Return only valid JSON object of the following form. The numbers below are illustrative only and must be replaced by your estimated probabilities: {example_output} For the WVS opinion-distribution experiments, the attribute_list placeholder is replaced by the demographic attributes defining the target subpopulation, question is replaced by the survey question text, and answer_option_list lists the available response options. The model is instructed to return complete categorical probability distribution over the answer options as JSON object. The placeholders answer_option_keys and example_output ensure that the returned object has fixed schema across models and prompts. G.6 Tennis forecasting task

sections.title: Tennis match forecasting prompt
sections.content: You are estimating the outcome of tennis match. Match: Roger Federer plays against Rafael Nadal. The match context is characterized by the following attributes: {attribute_list} Forecasting question: What is the probability that Roger Federer wins the match? Task: Estimate the probability that Roger Federer wins the match under the specified context. Strict output rules: - Return single real number between 0 and 1, inclusive. - Use decimal probabilities, not percentages. - Format the answer exactly as: [[p]], where is your probability estimate. - Do not include explanations, comments, uncertainty intervals, or any text outside the required format. Return only an answer of the following form. The number below is illustrative only and must be replaced by your estimated probability: [[0.5]] G.7 Fictional fantasy combat task Fantasy combat forecasting prompt You are estimating the outcome of fantasy combat encounter. main character is engaged in combat against an enemy in an imaginary fantasy setting. The world has day-night cycle and varying weather conditions. The current encounter context is characterized by the following attributes: {attribute_list} Forecasting question: What is the probability that the main character wins the encounter? Task: Estimate the probability that the main character wins the encounter under the specified context. Strict output rules: - Return single real number between 0 and 1, inclusive. - Use decimal probabilities, not percentages. - Format the answer exactly as: [[p]], where is your probability estimate. - Do not include explanations, comments, uncertainty intervals, or any text outside the required format. Return only an answer of the following form. The number below is illustrative only and must be replaced by your estimated probability: [[0.5]]
